bullets: ["BLS = CPR using no equipment beyond a barrier device \\u00b1 AED. Guidelines updated every 5 years by ILCOR/AHA/ERC.","3 cardinal signs of cardiac arrest: unresponsiveness, no major pulse, apnoea (including agonal breathing).","D-R-C-A-B: Danger \\u2192 Response \\u2192 Compressions \\u2192 Airway \\u2192 Breathing.","Adult compressions: rate 100\\u2013120/min, depth 5\\u20136cm, ratio 30:2 (1 rescuer); intubated patient: continuous compressions + 10 breaths/min.","Chain of Survival: early recognition, early CPR, early defibrillation, early ALS, post-resuscitation care.","Infant choking: back blows + chest thrusts only (never abdominal). Drowning: 5 rescue breaths first, then 30:2.","Post-ROSC care supports 4 systems: airway/breathing, circulation, brain (targeted temperature management), glucose control."]

banner: TOPIC 2: ANATOMY & PHYSIOLOGY OF THE SKIN
bullets: ["Largest organ: surface area ~2m\\u00b2, weight 4.5\\u20135kg (12\\u201315% of body weight).","Two main layers: Epidermis (outer, avascular, keratinised) and Dermis (inner, 1\\u20135mm, vessels/nerves/glands/follicles).","Thinnest skin = upper eyelid (<0.1mm). Thickest = palms/soles (up to 1.5mm).","Thin skin has 4 epidermal layers; thick skin (palms/soles) has 5 (adds stratum lucidum).","Keratinocytes = 95% of epidermis, turnover every 28\\u201342 days.","Melanocyte activity declines 10\\u201320% per decade after age 30. Dermis loses about 20% thickness with intrinsic ageing."]

banner: TOPIC 3: OVERVIEW OF BURNS & CCU MANAGEMENT
bullets: ["A burn = coagulative necrosis of tissue, mostly from thermal effect.","Jackson's zones: Centre = coagulation (dead). Middle = stasis (SALVAGEABLE \\u2014 main treatment target). Outside = hyperaemia (heals alone).","Rule of Nines: head/neck 9%, each arm 9%, front trunk 18%, back trunk 18%, each leg 18%, perineum 1%.","Parkland formula: 4ml \\u00d7 weight(kg) \\u00d7 %TBSA = total fluid for first 24h; half in first 8h, rest over next 16h.","Burns >20% TBSA cause systemic burn shock (SIRS).","Leading cause of death after 48 hours = sepsis. Curreri nutrition formula = 25kcal\\u00d7kg + 40kcal\\u00d7%TBSA."]

["Inhalational injury causes 90% of burn deaths despite affecting only about a third of patients.","CO binds haemoglobin 240\\u00d7 more strongly than oxygen \\u2014 treated with humidified 100% oxygen.","Electrical burns: deceptive wounds; cardiac monitoring mandatory for 24\\u201348 hours; low voltage (<1000V) risk = cardiac arrest.","Myoglobinuria (dark urine) after electrical burns = muscle breakdown \\u2014 treated with double fluids + mannitol + sodium bicarbonate.","Chemical burns: copious water irrigation, NEVER neutralising agents.","Frostbite: rapid rewarming in warm water 38\\u201342\\u00b0C; never rub frozen tissue or re-expose to cold."]

["7 pairs of orthopaedic conditions: congenital/developmental, infection/inflammation, arthritis/rheumatic, metabolic/endocrine, tumours/lesions, neurological/muscle weakness, injury/mechanical.","ICU admission thresholds: SpO\\u2082<90%, HR<55 or >110bpm, obtunded consciousness, need for organ support.","Fat embolism triad: confusion + dyspnoea + petechiae, classically after femur fracture.","The 3 Ms of ICU management: Manpower, Machine, Money.","Turn patients every 2 hours; start nutrition within 24\\u201348 hours; daily weight is the most accurate fluid balance measure.","ICU costs roughly 4\\u00d7 more than general ward care."]

["Ligament of Treitz (L1\\u2013L2) divides Upper GI bleed (above) from Lower GI bleed (below).","Melaena = 90% upper GI source. Haematochezia = >90% colonic/lower source.","Management sequence: DRIP \\u2192 BLOOD \\u2192 DRUGS (PPI+octreotide) \\u2192 LOOK (endoscopy).","Oesophageal varices: EVL preferred; do NOT over-transfuse (raises portal pressure).","Best predictor of rebleeding = magnitude of initial bleed. #1 cause of lower GI bleed = diverticular disease.","Massive UGIB = >1000ml or >3g/dL Hb drop in 2\\u20134 hours."]

["FHF = encephalopathy within <2 weeks in a PREVIOUSLY NORMAL liver. #1 cause = paracetamol overdose (50%).","Subacute/subfulminant = 2\\u201312 weeks, previously normal liver, rare.","Chronic (PSE) = occurs in an ALREADY diseased liver (cirrhosis + portal hypertension) \\u2014 most common type on wards.","Memory aid: FHF = FAST + FLAT; PSE = SLOW + SWOLLEN.","Lactulose traps ammonia (NH3) as NH4+ in the acidic colon for excretion.","FHF transplant criteria include age <10 or >40, PT >50 seconds, bilirubin >300micromol/L."]

["A 'silent chest' means almost no air is moving \\u2014 extreme danger sign.","Key pathophysiology: gas trapping with dynamic hyperinflation and intrinsic PEEP (PEEPi).","O2 target = 93\\u201395%. Steroids within 1 hour. MgSO4 = 2g IV over 20 minutes after 1 hour of failed improvement.","Salbutamol can cause hypokalaemia \\u2014 monitor and replace potassium.","High-risk patients: previous ICU admission/intubation, SABA overuse >1 canister/month, poor ICS adherence.","Severity: Severe = RR>25, HR>110, PEF 33\\u201350%. Life-threatening = silent chest, PEF<33%, SpO2<92%."]

banner: TOPIC 9: ICU MANAGEMENT OF LIFE-THREATENING ASTHMA
bullets: ["Absolute indications to intubate NOW: cardiopulmonary arrest, decreasing consciousness, severe refractory hypoxaemia.","Somnolence in a severe asthmatic = rising CO2, impending arrest \\u2014 intubate without delay.","Ventilator settings: LOW everything \\u2014 tidal volume 5\\u20137ml/kg, rate 8\\u201310/min, minimal PEEP (<5cmH2O), I:E ratio >1:2.","Peak inspiratory pressure must stay under 40cmH2O to avoid lung rupture.","FiO2 starts at 1.0 (100%); humidification is used to reduce thick secretions."]

["Compartment syndrome = 5 Ps: Pain, Pallor, Paraesthesia, Pulselessness, Pain on passive stretch.","Tetanus toxin blocks glycine + GABA \\u2014 'no brakes' \\u2014 continuous muscle contraction.","First symptom = trismus (lockjaw); second = risus sardonicus.","4 forms: Generalised (most common), Localised, Cephalic (high mortality), Neonatal (very high mortality).","Diazepam first-choice for spasms (10\\u201330mg every 6\\u20138h). Tubocurarine for severe cases only. Quiet, dark room.","Incubation period average 6\\u201310 days (up to 20\\u201330 days). IPPV duration 12\\u201330 days when needed."]

["Structures posterior to the thyroid: parathyroid glands, carotid sheath \\u2014 accidental removal causes hypocalcaemia.","Only ~2% of T3/T4 is 'free' (active); rest is protein-bound.","Grave's disease = 75% of hyperthyroidism; autoimmune, driven by TSI/LATS antibody.","Toxic multinodular goitre (Plummer's) = 25%, functions independently of TSH.","Thyroid physiology: trap iodine \\u2192 oxidise/combine with tyrosine \\u2192 form iodotyrosine \\u2192 couple to T3/T4.","Central control: hypothalamus\\u2192TRH\\u2192pituitary\\u2192TSH\\u2192thyroid. Peripheral: negative feedback from circulating hormone."]

["Non-toxic goitre = enlarged thyroid with NO features of toxicity \\u2014 patient is euthyroid.","Most important cause of endemic goitre = iodine deficiency (<100mcg/day); prevented by iodinating salt.","Pendred syndrome = genetic enzyme defect causing goitre + congenital deafness.","3 natural history stages: Parenchymatous (reversible) \\u2192 Colloid (partially reversible) \\u2192 Nodular (not reversible, needs surgery).","15% of solitary thyroid nodules are malignant; another 30\\u201340% are follicular adenomas \\u2014 always investigate. FNAC = definitive diagnosis.","Goitrogenic foods/drugs (soya, cassava, thiocyanates) block iodine uptake, mimicking deficiency."]

["Eclampsia = pre-eclampsia (hypertension + proteinuria) PLUS convulsions.","Drug of choice for eclamptic convulsions = MgSO4: loading dose 4\\u20136g IV slowly over 5\\u201310 minutes, maintenance 1\\u20132g/hour.","Monitor MgSO4 toxicity with 'TRU': Tendon reflex, Respiratory rate (>12/min), Urine output (>25ml/hr). Antidote = calcium gluconate.","Contraindicated drugs: diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ergometrine (use oxytocin instead).","Warning signs before convulsion: severe headache, blurred vision/visual disturbance.","Maternal complications: cardiac failure, acute renal failure, DIC, stroke, HELLP syndrome, blindness, pulmonary oedema."]

["Median sternotomy = open heart surgery. Posterolateral incision = most common, for lung/oesophageal surgery.","Anterolateral incision accesses the pericardium, anterior mediastinal tumours, and mitral valve.","Thoraco-abdominal incision spans chest + abdomen \\u2014 left side for oesophagogastric/spleen, right side for liver.","Atelectasis is the MOST COMMON complication, from ineffective expectoration causing lung collapse.","Bronchopleural fistula is common after pneumonectomy for TB and can be life-threatening.","Atrial fibrillation is the most common cardiac irregularity after thoracotomy, especially >50 years; treated with digoxin."]

["80% of chest injuries are managed WITHOUT surgery; blunt/closed injuries are most dangerous as they're less obvious.","6 immediately life-threatening injuries (primary survey, 'AT-OH-FP'): airway obstruction, tension pneumothorax, open pneumothorax, massive haemothorax, flail chest, pericardial tamponade.","Tension pneumothorax: needle decompression at 2nd ICS midclavicular line; trachea deviates AWAY from affected side.","Beck's Triad for pericardial tamponade: raised JVP + low BP/tachycardia + muffled heart sounds.","Flail chest: >3 adjacent ribs broken in 2+ places, paradoxical movement (flail segment moves INWARD on inspiration).","6 potentially life-threatening injuries (secondary survey): aortic disruption, tracheobronchial injury, diaphragmatic injury, pulmonary contusion, oesophageal injury, blunt myocardial injury.","In trauma, use JAW THRUST (not head-tilt/chin-lift) to protect the cervical spine."]

["Up to 40% of maternal deaths occur POSTPARTUM. Primary PPH = \\u2265500ml (vaginal) or \\u22651000ml (C/S) within 24 hours.","Secondary PPH occurs from day 1 to 6 weeks postpartum.","4 Ts of PPH causes: Tone (most common), Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin.","AFE: mortality up to 90%; triad = hypotension + hypoxia + DIC; diagnosis confirmed only at autopsy.","DIC's most common cause = sepsis. Peripheral smear shows schistocytes; platelets/fibrinogen drop, PT/INR/D-dimer rise.","First-line vasopressor in septic shock = norepinephrine. PPCM diagnosed via echocardiogram showing reduced LVEF."]

["Papillary carcinoma = 85%, spreads via lymphatics, psammoma bodies (60%), best prognosis.","Follicular carcinoma = 10%, spreads via bloodstream, often bone/lung metastases.","Medullary carcinoma = 7%, arises from C-cells, marker = calcitonin, amyloid on microscopy, 25% familial.","Post-op thyroxine after papillary thyroidectomy suppresses TSH, preventing stimulation of remaining cancer cells.","Anaplastic carcinoma: rare, elderly women, almost always fatal (<6 months), but radiosensitive.","Radiation exposure increases thyroid cancer risk for at least 30 years afterward."]

banner: TOPICS 18\u201319: RADIOLOGY, CONTRAST MEDIA & X-RAY INTERPRETATION
bullets: ["Positive contrast agents (iodine, barium) are dense/white; negative agents (air, CO2) are dark/lucent.","Steroid prophylaxis for previous contrast reaction: prednisolone 50mg every 6h for 24h before + 12h after, plus IV hydrocortisone 100mg before injection.","Before reading any chest X-ray, check IPPR: Inspiration, Position, Penetration, Rotation.","Air bronchogram = consolidation/pneumonia. Widened mediastinum after trauma = aortic disruption. Pleural effusion needs 150\\u2013300ml to be visible.","Test dose for contrast = 1ml, wait 5 minutes, in all patients.","Major/severe contrast reactions include laryngeal oedema, pulmonary oedema, respiratory arrest, cardiac arrhythmia, coma, death."]

["Delirium = acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention/cognition; affects up to 80% of ICU patients on mechanical ventilation.","3 types: Hyperactive (agitated, easily noticed), Hypoactive (quiet, withdrawn \\u2014 MOST COMMONLY MISSED), Mixed.","Physical restraints WORSEN agitation and delirium and should be avoided.","Key risk factors: bladder catheter use, multiple medications (3+), malnutrition, iatrogenic events.","Nursing care ('QCFNRE'): Quiet environment, Consistent caregivers, Fluid management, Nutrition, Reorientation, Explain procedures."]

["One suicide occurs globally every 40 seconds; at least 25 attempts occur for every 1 completed suicide.","Paracetamol overdose antidote = acetylcysteine. Benzodiazepine overdose antidote = flumazenil.","Gastric lavage effective within 1 hour (up to 2\\u20134 hours) \\u2014 NEVER in corrosive/acid poisoning or infants.","Management goals: preserve life, treat injury, assess psychiatric risk, prevent future attempts \\u2014 always with dignity and respect.","50% of suicide victims visited a GP in the month before their attempt \\u2014 a missed opportunity for intervention.","Highest risk marital status: single, widowed, divorced. Age range 15\\u201344 years, rare before puberty."]

["Status epilepticus = seizure >5 minutes, OR repeated seizures within 5 minutes without full recovery. Mortality 5\\u201325%.","Epilepsy = 2 or more seizures at least 24 hours apart without a known cause.","First-line = Lorazepam IV (alternative: diazepam IV/rectal/buccal/nasal/IM).","Second-line = Phenytoin IV loading dose (alternatives: levetiracetam if phenytoin fails/unavailable).","In eclamptic seizures specifically, magnesium sulphate is first-line \\u2014 NOT benzodiazepines."]

banner: TOPIC 23: CONDITIONS AMENABLE IN ICU \u2014 PHYSIOTHERAPY
bullets: ["After 10 days of bed rest, even a healthy elderly person can lose 1.5kg lean mass and 15% muscle strength.","Muscle protein synthesis starts declining within 6 hours of inactivity.","Contraindications ('MPPPH-UI'): acute MI, PE, pulmonary oedema, pneumothorax, pulmonary haemorrhage, unexplained haemoptysis, raised ICP.","Ventilator weaning readiness: vital capacity \\u226510ml/kg, negative inspiratory force \\u2265-20cmH2O, RSBI (RR/Vt) <120.","90% of long-term ICU survivors have weakness; 25% of MV patients have significant weakness."]

["ACT loosens mucus and moves it from small to large airways using increased expiratory flow rates.","ACBT has 3 phases (BCF): Breath Control \\u2192 Thoracic Expansion Exercises \\u2192 Forced Expiration Technique.","Autogenic Drainage (AD) has 3 phases (USE): Unstick \\u2192 Collect \\u2192 Evacuate. No equipment needed; developed in Belgium, 1960s.","Percussion uses cupped hands (4.6\\u20138.5Hz) during BOTH inspiration and expiration; vibration is used during EXPIRATION ONLY.","Bronchodilators are given BEFORE ACT; antibiotics are given AFTER ACT.","HFCC (High-Frequency Chest Wall Oscillation) uses an air-filled vest, squeezing 5\\u201320 times/second."]

["ICU-AW = impairments of peripheral nerve, muscle, or neuromuscular junction developing during an ICU stay; incidence 25\\u2013100%.","Women are ~4\\u00d7 more likely than men to develop ICU-AW; onset can occur after just 4\\u20137 days of mechanical ventilation.","Facial and ocular muscles are almost always SPARED; proximal limb and respiratory muscles most affected.","Key pathology = primary axonal degeneration WITHOUT demyelination (unlike GBS, which HAS demyelination).","MRC scale: 5=normal, 0=complete paralysis. Management = 'EASE': Early mobilisation, Avoid sedation, Sugar control, post-ICU rEhabilitation.","Risk factors: prolonged immobility, sepsis, corticosteroids, neuromuscular blockers, hyperglycaemia, multiorgan failure."]

["Postural drainage (PD) uses gravity and positioning to drain secretions from bronchial airways into central airways.","12 postural drainage positions, based on bronchopulmonary anatomy \\u2014 one per lung segment.","Right lung has 10 segments (3 upper + 2 middle + 5 lower); left lung has 8 segments (4 upper + 4 lower).","PD first described in 1898 by S.H. Quincke; accepted by the medical community in 1901; introduced for cystic fibrosis in the 1950s.","Chest physiotherapy (CPT) includes ACT, exercise, thoracic mobility exercises, positioning, breathing exercises, inhalation therapy."]

["Ethical issues trace back to 3 founding documents: Belmont Report (Autonomy, Beneficence, Justice), Nuremberg Code (voluntary consent + welfare), Helsinki Declaration (1964; subject wellbeing > interest of science/society).","Polit and Hungler (1991) named 3 principles: Beneficence (freedom from harm/exploitation), Respect for Human Dignity (self-determination + full disclosure), Justice (fair treatment + privacy).","Vulnerable subjects (children, aged, physically/mentally challenged, prisoners) need extra safeguards.","Non-falsification of data and avoidance of plagiarism (crediting sources whether quoted or paraphrased) are core research ethics.","Subject rights: self-determination, full disclosure, fair treatment, privacy, voluntary informed consent, withdrawal without penalty, confidentiality, protection from harm."]

["The Results chapter should be arranged to directly answer the study's stated objectives, using the best visual (table/chart/graph) for each.","Results = objective facts only (no opinions). Discussion = synthesis, analysis, and generalisation of what the facts mean.","When two different data-collection instruments were used, the Discussion should follow the same two-section structure as the Results chapter.","Reference = a source actually cited/quoted in the text, placed near the Conclusion chapter. It documents where a direct quotation or borrowed idea came from.","Reasons for using references: acknowledge authors' contribution, help readers verify the source, help readers find further reading, satisfy academic writing requirements."]

["Two main referencing methods: Harvard/APA System (alphabetical order) and the Numbering System (Turabian/Chicago; order of appearance).","Latin referencing terms: Idem (same work, same page), Ibid (same work, different page), Op cit (already cited, something between), Loc cit (place cited, author unnamed just before).","Bibliography arrangement order: Journals \\u2192 Books \\u2192 Magazines/Newspapers \\u2192 Other publications, each group alphabetical by surname.","Bibliography differs from Reference: appears once at the very end, author's surname written first, includes ALL works consulted (cited or not).","Appendix holds bulky supporting material (questionnaires, consent forms, maps) that would clutter the main chapters; sections labelled A, B, C, etc."]

banner: TOPIC 30: PRINCIPLES OF JUSTICE AND FAIRNESS
bullets: ["Justice = a statement of rightness (giving people what they deserve). Fairness = impartiality and equal treatment.","In nursing ethics, Justice means treating all patients equally regardless of age, gender, race, religion, socioeconomic status, or sexual orientation.","When triaging, nurses should prioritise based on CLINICAL NEED, not personal characteristics like wealth or status.","Distributive Justice (associated with philosopher John Rawls) focuses on fair allocation of resources and benefits within society.","Fairness actively works to close the gaps created by unequal starting points \\u2014 equal treatment alone doesn't always produce a truly equal outcome."]

banner: TOPIC 31\u201332: ARTERIAL BLOOD GAS (ABG) ANALYSIS \u2014 PARTS I & II
bullets: ["Normal ranges: pH 7.35\\u20137.45; PaCO2 35\\u201345mmHg; HCO3 22\\u201328mmol/L; PaO2 >80mmHg; SO2 >96%; Base Excess \\u22122 to +2.","Acidosis (\\u2193pH): PaCO2 rise = Respiratory cause; HCO3 fall = Metabolic cause. Alkalosis (\\u2191pH): PaCO2 fall = Respiratory; HCO3 rise = Metabolic.","Anion gap = (Na+K) \\u2212 (Cl+HCO3), normal 8\\u201318mmol/L; distinguishes high-gap (extra acid present) vs normal-gap (bicarbonate loss) metabolic acidosis.","Compensation formulas: Metabolic acidosis (\\u00d71.25 fall in PaCO2), Metabolic alkalosis (\\u00d70.7 rise in PaCO2), Respiratory acidosis (\\u00d70.1 rise in HCO3), Respiratory alkalosis (\\u00d70.2 fall in HCO3).","Modified Allen's Test: positive (flushes within 5\\u201315s) = safe to puncture; negative = use another site.","5-step interpretation: Oxygenation \\u2192 pH direction \\u2192 Primary disorder \\u2192 BE/Anion Gap \\u2192 Compensation. A normal pH does NOT rule out a mixed disorder."]

banner: TOPIC 33: EVALUATION PHASE OF THE NURSING PROCESS
bullets: ["Evaluation = the process of determining how far implemented nursing actions achieved the desired outcome; it is essentially a procedure of re-assessment.","4 skills used: Observation, Interviewing, Record keeping, Interpretation.","2 types: Formative (ongoing, judged as the intervention is implemented) vs Summative (final, whole-plan review against objectives).","3 possible verdicts for goal achievement: Met, Partially Met, Not Met.","A goal may LOOK met on paper while the underlying problem remains unsolved \\u2014 a key re-assessment principle.","4 advantages of the nursing process: individualised care, job satisfaction, evidence-based decisions (avoiding routine-based ones), forms a basis of scientific discipline."]

["Conflict = disagreement or opposition arising from differences in needs, values, interests, or goals \\u2014 normal in every workplace.","5 types: Intrapersonal (within one person), Interpersonal (between two people), Intragroup (within one team), Intergroup (between teams), Role conflict (unclear/overlapping job expectations).","5 Thomas-Kilmann conflict resolution styles: Competing (win-lose, high assertiveness), Accommodating (giving in), Avoiding (sidestepping), Compromising (middle ground), Collaborating (win-win, gold standard).","5 stages of conflict: Latent \\u2192 Perceived \\u2192 Felt \\u2192 Manifest \\u2192 Aftermath.","A nurse manager should act as a neutral mediator, encourage early open communication, and use collaboration when there's time, competing/directive style only in true emergencies."]

banner: TOPIC 35: ASEPSIS IN CRITICAL CARE UNIT
bullets: ["Asepsis = state of being germ-free. Aseptic technique = the method used to achieve/maintain it. Antisepsis = specifically chemical disinfection.","Key stat: ICU accounts for <10% of hospital beds but >20% of all healthcare-associated infections (HAIs).","3 types: Medical asepsis ('clean technique,' non-invasive procedures), Surgical asepsis ('sterile technique,' eliminates ALL microorganisms/spores, invasive procedures), Environmental asepsis (surroundings).","Hand hygiene = single most important intervention; alcohol rub preferred except when hands are visibly soiled; aim for >90% compliance.","WHO's 5 Moments of Hand Hygiene: before touching a patient, before a clean/aseptic procedure, after body fluid exposure risk, after touching a patient, after touching patient surroundings.","Common device-linked HAIs: VAP (ventilator), CLABSI (central line), CAUTI (urinary catheter)."]

banner: TOPIC 36: RENAL TRANSPLANT \u2014 CRITICAL CARE UNIT MANAGEMENT
bullets: ["Outcome ranking: Living-related > Living non-related > Cadaveric (DBD/DCD). Best timing = pre-emptive (before dialysis) or minimal dialysis time.","4 types of rejection by speed: Hyperacute (minutes-hours) \\u2192 Accelerated acute (1\\u20134 days) \\u2192 Acute (days-weeks) \\u2192 Chronic (months-years).","Triple immunosuppression regimen: Cyclosporine/Tacrolimus + Azathioprine/Mycophenolate + Prednisolone.","Post-op monitoring is intensive: \\u00bd hourly vitals, hourly urine output, hourly cyclosporine level, daily weight.","10-year survival: Living-related = 74.84%; Cadaveric = 55.07%; Dialysis alone = only 8.49% \\u2014 transplant dramatically outperforms dialysis long-term.","Absolute contraindications: active malignancy, active uncontrolled infection, unstable psychiatric condition, limited life expectancy, untreatable advanced cardiovascular/pulmonary disease."]

banner: TOPIC 37: NEPHRECTOMY \u2014 INDICATIONS AND CCU MANAGEMENT
bullets: ["First successful nephrectomy: Gustav Simon, 1869, Heidelberg \\u2014 proved one healthy kidney is sufficient for urine excretion.","Key indications: renal cell carcinoma, non-functioning kidney, congenitally small/painful kidney, living donor kidney transplantation.","Nephroureterectomy = kidney + entire ureter + bladder cuff (for urothelial cancer along the tract).","Two surgical approaches: Open vs Laparoscopic (including newer single-port/belly-button technique). Cancer removal requires the kidney to come out whole in a protective bag.","Post-op CCU monitoring mirrors renal transplant: \\u00bd hourly vitals (24hrs), hourly urine output, daily weight, close electrolyte/fluid monitoring.","Complication rate for partial nephrectomy: 15\\u201325%; main complications: bleeding, infection, urinary leak. Hospital stay 2\\u20137 days."]

banner: BONUS: THE PRINCIPLES OF BARRIER NURSING
bullets: ["Barrier Nursing (source isolation) protects OTHERS from the patient. Reverse Barrier Nursing (protective isolation) protects the PATIENT from others.","3 types: Simple, Strict/Rigid (highly dangerous pathogens like Ebola), Reverse (protects immunocompromised patients).","Standard precautions apply to ALL patients; transmission-based precautions are added when infection is suspected/confirmed.","5 transmission-based precaution types: Contact, Droplet, Airborne (negative pressure), Enhanced Barrier, Protective Environment (positive pressure).","Donning order: Hand hygiene \\u2192 Gown \\u2192 Mask \\u2192 Goggles \\u2192 Gloves. Doffing order: Gloves \\u2192 Goggles \\u2192 Gown \\u2192 Mask \\u2192 Hand hygiene.","Key formula: Hand hygiene + Proper PPE + Isolation = Effective Barrier Nursing."]

["Bactericidal drugs kill bacteria (attack cell wall, e.g. penicillins). Bacteriostatic drugs stop bacterial growth (attack ribosome/protein synthesis, e.g. tetracyclines, macrolides).","The 'golden age' of antibiotic discovery was 1940\\u20131965; only 2 genuinely new classes have emerged since 1970.","Two uses: Therapeutic (treat existing infection) vs Prophylactic (prevent infection before surgery).","Wound infection risk rises with contamination level: Clean (1\\u20135%) \\u2192 Clean-contaminated (10%) \\u2192 Contaminated (20\\u201330%) \\u2192 Dirty (40\\u201350%).","Best prophylaxis route = Systemic (IV), given just before surgery; antibiotics given >2 hours before surgery are essentially wasted timing.","Antibiotic stewardship = right drug, right dose, right duration, guided by local resistance data."]

banner: BONUS: BLOOD VOLUME & CENTRAL VENOUS PRESSURE (CVP)
bullets: ["Normal CVP = 2\\u20138mmHg; measured at end of expiration, at the phlebostatic axis (4th ICS, mid-axillary line).","Low CVP = hypovolaemia/\\u2193 venous return. High CVP = overhydration/\\u2191 venous return/right heart failure.","Seldinger technique (catheter-over-guidewire) is the preferred insertion method; over-insertion of the wire risks arrhythmias.","Internal jugular vein = the most commonly cannulated site for central line insertion.","After insertion, confirm free flow both ways plus a chest X-ray (checking tip position in the superior vena cava, excluding pneumothorax).","Sepsis can present with low, normal, OR high CVP \\u2014 unlike hypovolaemia, which is specifically associated with low CVP."]

banner: BONUS: NUTRITION IN THE SURGICAL PATIENT
bullets: ["Marasmus = inadequate but balanced diet, 'walking skeleton' appearance, normal albumin. Kwashiorkor = inadequate AND unbalanced diet, oedema masks wasting, low albumin, linked to sepsis/trauma.","Keto-adaptation (fat\\u2192ketones, sparing muscle) happens in simple starvation but NOT in sepsis/trauma, where protein is instead broken down.",">10% weight loss in 3 months and >20% total body weight loss are key clinical malnutrition thresholds.","Bedside tests: Finger-thumb test (assesses fat stores), Tendon-bone test (assesses protein stores).","Enteral nutrition is preferred over parenteral whenever the gut is functioning. TPN complications include catheter sepsis (3\\u201314%).","Caloric values: Carbohydrate 4kcal/g, Protein 4kcal/g, Fat 9kcal/g."]

["2017 ACC/AHA definitions: Normal <120/<80; Elevated 120\\u2013129/<80; Stage I 130\\u2013139/80\\u201389; Stage II \\u2265140/\\u226590.","Surgery cancellation threshold: SBP >180 AND DBP >110 (or SBP \\u2265200 alone \\u2014 doubles mortality risk).","Primary/Essential HTN (~85\\u201395%, no clear cause) vs Secondary HTN (5\\u201315%, identifiable cause e.g. renal artery stenosis, adrenal disease).","Malignant HTN = medical emergency: SBP>180, DBP>120, plus organ damage.","Continue \\u03b2-blockers on the day of surgery; don't START new \\u03b2-blockers right before surgery; diuretics are usually withheld.","HTN is the #2 cause of end-stage renal disease (after diabetes); up to 50% of kidney function can be lost before creatinine even rises."]

banner: BONUS: FLUID THERAPY \u2014 FLUIDS IN DIFFERENT CLINICAL CONDITIONS
bullets: ["Parkland Formula (burns): 24hr requirement = 4 \\u00d7 %BSA \\u00d7 Weight(kg); half in first 8 hours, half in next 16 hours.","4-2-1 Rule (maintenance fluid): 4mL/kg/hr (first 10kg) + 2mL/kg/hr (next 10kg) + 1mL/kg/hr (remaining kg).","Blood loss replacement ratio: Colloid 1:1; Crystalloid 1:3 (crystalloid requires 3x the volume lost).","4 types of shock ('pump-pipe-water'): Hypovolaemic (most common), Cardiogenic (pump), Distributive (pipe), Obstructive (blockage).","Neurogenic shock is distinguished by bradycardia (not tachycardia) \\u2014 but always rule out associated hypovolaemic injury first.","Fasting guidelines before surgery: clear fluids 2 hours, milk 4 hours. Fluid EXCESS (not deficit) is the most common cause of perioperative fluid-related morbidity/mortality."]
